The plane has just landed in Lisbon.
I checked in in Sao Paulo this morning.
Right now, I am looking for the train.
Then I am going to take it to the hotel.
I have just landed, and I have already gone through passport control.
One step at a time.
The airport is big, but I am doing it one step at a time.
You have had the facts for fourteen lessons. Today you learn to move between the tenses without stopping.
Good afternoon, welcome to Lisbon. You look a little lost, can I help you find something?
Yes, please. I have just landed from Sao Paulo, and I am on my own. I am looking for the train to the city.
Of course. Did you check in for your bag, or is it carry-on only?
I checked in a bag in Sao Paulo this morning. I have already gone through passport control, but my suitcase hasn't come out yet.
Then collect the bag first, belt four, and then come back this way for the train. Do you know which hotel you are going to?
Yes, the Hotel Baixa, in the center. I am going to take the train, and then I am going to walk from the station.
Good plan. The train is clean and cheap, and it goes straight there. One tip, keep track of your platform number, because it can change.
I will. One more thing, my phone has no signal. I need to sort out a SIM card right away, for the maps.
There is a phone shop just past the bags, on the left. Buy the SIM there, and they will set it up for you. Then you are ready.
Thank you. So, the bag, then the SIM card, then the train. Let me double-check, belt four, and the train is this way?
Exactly right. You are doing all of this on your own, senhora, and you are handling it beautifully. Welcome to Lisbon. Have a smooth trip.
I have just landed, and I am on my own.
I have already gone through passport control.
Right now I am looking for the train to the city.
I am on my own, but I can handle it, one step at a time.

Good afternoon, and welcome to Lisbon. My name is Bruno, and I work here in the arrivals hall, so let me walk you through the next hour, because a lot of you are traveling alone for the first time, and the airport is big, and you are tired. First, the good news: the hard part is behind you. Your plane has already landed. You have already done the eleven hours. What is left is small, and it is all in one line. So here is the line. One: you have just come off the plane, so follow the purple signs to passport control. Have your passport open and ready in your hand before you get there. Two: after passport control, go down to baggage, and find your belt number on the screen. If your bag has not come out yet, do not panic. Wait. It always comes. Three: when you have your bag, walk straight out through the green channel, unless you have something to declare. Four, and this is the one people get wrong: do not take the first taxi that shouts at you. Keep track of your money. The train and the metro are cheaper, they are clean, and they go straight to the city. Now, one more thing, and then I will let you go. Some of you are worried because you are on your own, and there is nobody to translate. Listen to me. You do not need perfect English to get from this hall to your hotel. You need six words: passport, bag, train, hotel, please, and thank you. That is the whole journey. You have already flown across an ocean by yourself. You can handle the last hour. Follow the signs, keep your passport close, and welcome to Portugal.

My name is Inês, I am seventy-one, and I want to tell you about the first time I did a whole trip on my own, because for forty years I never did. My husband did everything. He checked us in, he talked at passport control, he found the taxi, he asked for the room. I just followed him, with my hands folded, like a good quiet wife, and I saw the world through the back of his coat. And then he was gone, and my daughter said, Mother, you cannot travel now, you do not speak English, you will get lost. And something in me stood up. So I booked a flight to Porto, alone, and I did not tell her until the morning I left. I will be honest with you: at the airport in São Paulo my hands were shaking. But I had made a little card, and on the card I had written every step in order. Check in. Go through security. Find the gate. Board. And I did them, one at a time, like climbing stairs, and I did not look at the whole staircase, only the next step. In Porto I went through passport control, I collected my bag, I found the train, and I checked into my hotel, and the young man at the desk had no idea that he was the first person in a foreign country I had ever spoken to without my husband beside me. When I closed the door of that room, I sat on the bed, and I cried, and then I laughed, because the trip had been completely smooth, and the only hard part, the whole time, had been believing I could. So if you are a woman my age, and everybody is telling you what you cannot do, let me tell you what I know. You have handled harder things than an airport. Make your little card. Take the next step. And go.
